source: Learning Ansible with Rocky - Ansible Basics: Playbooks, collections, and validation

questions.q: What format are Ansible playbooks written in?
questions.why: Playbooks are YAML files grouping a set of tasks.

questions.q: In a play, what does `become: true` do?
options: Runs the tasks as another user (root by default) via privilege escalation
Marks the play as a handler
questions.why: become escalates privilege; become_user sets which user (root by default).

questions.q: ansible-playbook exits 0. Besides success, what else can that mean (a documented gotcha)?
options: The run was interrupted by the user
questions.why: Ansible returns OK/0 even when no host matches your target, which can mislead you.

questions.q: Which exit code means "one or more hosts are unreachable"?
questions.why: 0 ok/no match, 1 error, 2 hosts failing, 3 hosts unreachable, 4 parse error.

questions.q: Which collection provides the default built-in modules (so dnf may be written ansible.builtin.dnf)?
questions.why: Default modules live in ansible.builtin; other collections are installed separately.

questions.q: Install the collection community.mysql with ansible-galaxy.
questions.why: ansible-galaxy collection install <name> fetches a collection of roles/modules/plugins.

questions.q: Check the syntax of a playbook play.yml without running it.
questions.why: --syntax-check validates the playbook without executing any task.

questions.q: Which tool does the guide use to lint the YAML of your playbooks?
questions.why: dnf install yamllint, then yamllint test.yml checks the YAML syntax.
